title: Security Basics Every Dev Must Know
subtitle: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, rate limiting, and OWASP Top 10

sections.title: SQL Injection
sections.text:
SQL injection occurs when user input is directly concatenated into SQL queries. An attacker can input '; DROP TABLE users; -- to delete tables, or ' OR '1'='1 to bypass authentication.

The fix is simple: parameterized queries (prepared statements). Never interpolate user input directly into SQL strings. ORMs like Prisma, Sequelize, and TypeORM handle this automatically, but raw queries still need care.

Always validate and sanitize inputs. A good rule: trust nothing from the client.

annotations.text: Input treated as data, not code

sections.caption: Parameterized queries treat user input as data, never as executable SQL
sections.callout: SQL injection is the #1 web application vulnerability. Always use parameterized queries. ORMs help but raw SQL still needs preparation.

sections.title: XSS, CSRF & Common Attacks
sections.text:
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) — Attacker injects JavaScript into a page viewed by other users. Stored XSS (saved in database, executed on load) and Reflected XSS (in URL, executed once). Prevent by escaping all user-generated content.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) — Attacker tricks a logged-in user into performing an action (like changing their email) without consent. Prevent with anti-CSRF tokens or SameSite cookies.

Rate Limiting — Limit requests per IP to prevent brute force attacks, DDoS, and API abuse. Implement using token bucket or sliding window algorithm. Redis is commonly used for distributed rate limiting.

items.heading: XSS
items.body: Injected scripts. Prevent: escape output, CSP headers, sanitize HTML.

items.heading: CSRF
items.body: Cross-site request forgery. Prevent: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies.

items.heading: Rate Limiting
items.body: Limit requests/IP. Token bucket algorithm. Use Redis for distributed limits.

items.heading: Clickjacking
items.body: Framing attacks. Prevent: X-Frame-Options: DENY header.

items.heading: Brute Force
items.body: Repeated login attempts. Prevent: rate limit + account lockout + CAPTCHA.

items.heading: Dependency Vulns
items.body: Vulnerable packages. Prevent: npm audit, Dependabot, lock files.

sections.caption: Six common security threats and how to prevent them

sections.title: OWASP Top 10 & Security Mindset
sections.text:
The OWASP Top 10 is the standard awareness document for web application security. The current top risks include:

  1. Broken Access Control — Users accessing data they shouldn't
  2. Cryptographic Failures — Weak encryption, exposed secrets
  3. Injection — SQL, NoSQL, OS command injection
  4. Insecure Design — Missing security in architecture
  5. Security Misconfiguration — Default passwords, verbose errors

Security isn't a feature — it's a mindset. Never trust input. Principle of least privilege. Encrypt everything in transit (HTTPS/TLS). Hash passwords. Keep dependencies updated. Log suspicious activity.
sections.caption: OWASP Top 10 — the most critical web application security risks
sections.callout: Subscribe to GitHub Dependabot alerts. Run npm audit / pip audit regularly. Use a secrets scanner (git-secrets, truffleHog). Enable 2FA on all accounts.
